questions: A well-known early-2000s beginner’s guide to UNIX and Linux commands, authored by a UK academic and made freely available for download and modification, is released under a specific Creative Commons license that predates the 4.0 suite. Identify the exact Creative Commons license (including version) under which such tutorials were commonly published at that time when marked “Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike.” Then, based on the official license deed, explain in detail: (1) the freedoms granted to users, (2) the conditions users must follow, (3) any clauses about additional restrictions or revocation, (4) disclaimers regarding warranties or other rights, and (5) what the Creative Commons organization recommends regarding newer versions of this license.

An older educational resource on UNIX and Linux states that it is released under an earlier version of the Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike license and recommends that new works instead use the current 4.0 International version of that same license family. Identify the full name of the recommended 4.0 license and provide its canonical URL. Then, drawing from the official license deed and Creative Commons’ explanation of version 4.0, summarize: (1) the core freedoms granted to users, (2) all primary conditions imposed on reuse, (3) the key legal disclaimers provided, and (4) at least four major improvements introduced in version 4.0 compared to prior versions, particularly regarding international enforceability, database rights, attribution practices, moral/publicity rights, and license violation remedies.

rounds.question: A well-known early-2000s beginner’s guide to UNIX and Linux commands, authored by a UK academic and made freely available for download and modification, is released under a specific Creative Commons license that predates the 4.0 suite. Identify the exact Creative Commons license (including version) under which such tutorials were commonly published at that time when marked “Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike.” Then, based on the official license deed, explain in detail: (1) the freedoms granted to users, (2) the conditions users must follow, (3) any clauses about additional restrictions or revocation, (4) disclaimers regarding warranties or other rights, and (5) what the Creative Commons organization recommends regarding newer versions of this license.
checklist_width: Correct identification of freedoms: Share (copy and redistribute) and Adapt (remix, transform, build upon)
Statement that freedoms are irrevocable provided terms are followed
Attribution requirement (credit, link to license, indicate changes)
NonCommercial restriction (no commercial use)
ShareAlike requirement (derivatives under same license)
No additional restrictions clause (no legal/technological barriers beyond license terms)
Disclaimer of warranties and note about other rights (e.g., privacy, publicity, moral rights)
Mention that version 4.0 is recommended as more internationally robust and user-friendly
checklist_depth: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0) correctly identified as the pre-4.0 license matching the described tutorial
Logical link: Early-2000s UNIX/Linux tutorial marked 'Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike' and predating 4.0 implies CC BY-NC-SA 2.0
rounds.rationale: The question uses contextual clues (early-2000s UNIX tutorial, UK academic author, Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike, pre-4.0) to require logical identification of the specific Creative Commons license version (Deep reasoning). It then demands aggregation of multiple dispersed elements from the official license deed—freedoms, conditions, revocation terms, disclaimers, and upgrade recommendations—ensuring broad information synthesis (Wide scope).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Notice This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust . If
rounds.answer_a:
The early-2000s “Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike” Creative Commons license in wide use for free academic tutorials (including UK-authored UNIX/Linux guides) was typically **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0** (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0). [1][2]

1. **Freedoms granted**  
   Users are free to **share** (copy, distribute, display, and perform the work) and **adapt** (make derivative works), in any medium or format, for **noncommercial** purposes, provided they follow the license terms. [1]

2. **Conditions**  
   - **Attribution**: Must give appropriate credit, provide the license notice, and indicate if changes were made, without implying endorsement.  
   - **NonCommercial**: No use “primarily intended for or directed toward commercial advantage or private monetary compensation.” [1]  
   - **ShareAlike**: Derivatives must be distributed under the **same** or a **compatible** license. [1]

3. **Additional restrictions / revocation**  
   Users may not impose legal terms or technological measures (e.g., DRM) that restrict others from exercising the license’s permissions. [1] Rights are automatically terminated if terms are violated, but may be reinstated if the violation is cured. (Termination/reinstatement language is elaborated more clearly in later 3.0+ licenses.)

4. **Disclaimers**  
   The license is provided **“as-is”** with **no warranties**, and it does not affect moral rights, publicity/privacy rights, or fair dealing/fair use and other exceptions. [1][2]

5. **CC recommendations on newer versions**  
   Creative Commons now recommends using **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0** for new works, describing 4.0 as the most up-to-date, internationally robust version. [3]

## References  
[1] Creative Commons, “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 Generic (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0) – Deed”  
[2] Creative Commons, Legal Code: CC BY-NC-SA 2.0  
[3] Creative Commons, “About The Licenses / License Versions”
rounds.answer_b:
The tutorial in question is "UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners" by Michael Stonebank at the University of Surrey, commonly published under the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0)** license [1][2].

**(1) Freedoms Granted:**
Users are free to **share** (copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format) and **adapt** (remix, transform, and build upon the material). These freedoms apply for any purpose that is non-commercial [3].

**(2) Conditions Users Must Follow:**
Three conditions apply. **Attribution (BY):** Users must give appropriate credit to the original author, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made, in any reasonable manner but not suggesting endorsement. **NonCommercial (NC):** Users may not use the material for commercial purposes. **ShareAlike (SA):** If users remix, transform, or build upon the material, they must distribute their contributions under the same or a compatible license [3].

**(3) Additional Restrictions and Revocation:**
Users may not apply legal terms or technological measures (such as DRM) that legally restrict others from doing anything the license permits. The licensor cannot revoke freedoms as long as the user follows the license terms [3].

**(4) Disclaimers Regarding Warranties and Other Rights:**
The license does not guarantee warranties of any kind. It also does not address publicity, privacy, or moral rights. Patent and trademark rights are not granted. The licensor is not required to ensure all uses are free of third-party claims [3].

**(5) Recommendations Regarding Newer Versions:**
Creative Commons officially recommends that users consider re-licensing under the **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0** version, as newer versions contain improved language, better international applicability, and enhanced legal clarity. A notice on the 2.0 deed directs users to the latest version [3][4].

## References
[1] Stonebank, M. "UNIX Tutorial for Beginners." University of Surrey. http://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[2] Creative Commons. "Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 Generic." https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/
[3] Creative Commons. "CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 Legal Code." https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/legalcode
[4] Creative Commons. "License Versions." https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/licensing-considerations/version4/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identify the license as Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike 2.0 (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0), logically linking the early-2000s timeframe and pre-4.0 status. Both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both cover the required checklist items—freedoms (Share and Adapt), irrevocability (conditioned on compliance), Attribution, NonCommercial, ShareAlike, no additional restrictions (no DRM/legal barriers), disclaimers (no warranties; other rights like moral/publicity), and recommendation of 4.0 as more robust. Agent B is slightly more explicit about irrevocability (“cannot revoke freedoms as long as terms are followed”) and adds helpful detail about patent/trademark rights and third-party claims, improving completeness.

Finally, Presentation & User Experience: Both are clearly structured and scannable. However, Agent B provides slightly richer contextual grounding (naming the specific tutorial), clearer condition breakdowns, and more detailed disclaimer language. Citation density is also marginally higher. Since both are factually strong and well formatted, the distinction is based on marginal gains in clarity and comprehensiveness.

Therefore, Agent B offers a slightly better overall user experience, but Agent A has no substantive failures.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: An older educational resource on UNIX and Linux states that it is released under an earlier version of the Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike license and recommends that new works instead use the current 4.0 International version of that same license family. Identify the full name of the recommended 4.0 license and provide its canonical URL. Then, drawing from the official license deed and Creative Commons’ explanation of version 4.0, summarize: (1) the core freedoms granted to users, (2) all primary conditions imposed on reuse, (3) the key legal disclaimers provided, and (4) at least four major improvements introduced in version 4.0 compared to prior versions, particularly regarding international enforceability, database rights, attribution practices, moral/publicity rights, and license violation remedies.
checklist_width: Canonical URL: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
Freedom to Share (copy and redistribute in any medium or format)
Freedom to Adapt (remix, transform, and build upon the material)
Condition: Attribution (credit, link to license, indicate changes, reasonable manner, no endorsement implication)
Condition: NonCommercial (no commercial use)
Condition: ShareAlike (derivatives must use same license)
Condition: No additional restrictions (no legal/technological restrictions beyond license)
Disclaimer: No warranties given
Disclaimer: Deed is not a license and has no legal value
Disclaimer: Creative Commons is not a law firm and provides no legal services
Improvement: Globally enforceable, no porting required, official translations
Improvement: Explicit coverage of sui generis database rights
Improvement: Clearer treatment/waiver of moral, publicity, and personality rights
Improvement: 30-day window to cure license violations with automatic reinstatement
Improvement: Clearer attribution flexibility (e.g., link to separate attribution page)
Improvement: Increased readability and simplified structure
checklist_depth: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0) + Identified as the recommended newer version replacing earlier 2.0/3.0 versions
Connection to Creative Commons official deed page and 4.0 explanatory materials demonstrating knowledge of both the license terms and the documented improvements in version 4.0
rounds.rationale: The question uses the logic of an older UNIX/Linux tutorial referencing an outdated BY-NC-SA license and recommending the 4.0 version, without naming the specific document (Deep). The respondent must identify the correct 4.0 license and aggregate information from both the official license deed (freedoms, conditions, disclaimers) and Creative Commons’ version 4.0 overview page (improvements and legal clarifications), requiring synthesis across multiple authoritative sources (Wide).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Canonical URL https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/ See the legal code You are free to: Share — copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format Adapt —
rounds.answer_a:
The recommended license is **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**. Its canonical URL is: **https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/**. [1]

1. **Core freedoms**  
   CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 allows anyone to **share** (copy and redistribute) and **adapt** (remix, transform, and build upon) the material in any medium or format, for **noncommercial purposes**, worldwide and for the term of copyright. These freedoms are irrevocable so long as the license terms are followed. [1]

2. **Primary conditions**  
   - **Attribution**: Give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made, in a reasonable manner and without implying endorsement. [1]  
   - **NonCommercial**: No use “primarily intended for or directed toward commercial advantage or monetary compensation.” [1]  
   - **ShareAlike**: If you remix, transform, or build upon the material, you must distribute your contributions under the **same license** (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0) or a permitted compatible license. [1]  
   - **No additional restrictions**: You may not apply legal terms or technological measures that legally restrict others from exercising the license’s permissions. [1]

3. **Key disclaimers**  
   The material is provided **“as-is”** with **no warranties**; licensors may not be held liable. The license does not affect moral rights, publicity/privacy rights, or limitations and exceptions such as fair use/fair dealing. [1][2]

4. **Major improvements in 4.0**  
   - Fully **internationalized**, replacing many ported, jurisdiction-specific versions. [2]  
   - Explicit coverage and treatment of **sui generis database rights**, granting similar freedoms and conditions where such rights exist. [2]  
   - Clearer, more flexible **attribution** requirements, allowing credit in reasonable, customary ways and supporting attribution via links. [1][2]  
   - More explicit handling of **moral and publicity/privacy rights**, clarifying that they are not licensed but also not overridden. [2]  
   - Improved, more forgiving **license violation remedies**, including automatic reinstatement of rights upon timely curing of breaches. [2]

## References  
[1] Creative Commons, “Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International – Deed,” https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/  
[2] Creative Commons, “Version 4.0 – What’s New,” https://creativecommons.org/version4/
rounds.answer_b:
The recommended license is **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**, with the canonical URL: **https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/** [1].

## (1) Core Freedoms Granted

Users are free to **Share** — copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format — and **Adapt** — remix, transform, and build upon the material. These freedoms apply as long as the use is non-commercial. The licensor cannot revoke these freedoms while the user complies with the license terms [1].

## (2) Primary Conditions on Reuse

Three conditions apply. **Attribution (BY):** Users must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made, in any reasonable manner without implying endorsement. **NonCommercial (NC):** The material may not be used for commercial purposes. **ShareAlike (SA):** Adaptations must be distributed under the same or a compatible license. Additionally, users may not apply legal terms or technological measures (e.g., DRM) that restrict others from exercising licensed rights [1][2].

## (3) Key Legal Disclaimers

The license is provided "as-is" with no warranties of any kind, including merchantability or fitness for a particular purpose. It does not grant trademark or patent rights. Moral rights, publicity, and privacy rights are not addressed by the license. The licensor is not liable for any damages arising from use of the material [2][3].

## (4) Major Improvements in Version 4.0

First, **international enforceability**: version 4.0 is a single "International" license replacing jurisdiction-specific ports, making it globally applicable without localized legal adaptations [4]. Second, **database rights (sui generis)**: version 4.0 explicitly covers database rights recognized in the EU and other jurisdictions, which prior versions did not address [4][5]. Third, **improved attribution practices**: licensees may now satisfy attribution by linking to a separate page containing required attribution information, simplifying compliance [4]. Fourth, **moral and publicity rights**: the license clarifies that moral rights are waived only to the extent necessary to exercise licensed rights, improving predictability [4]. Fifth, **automatic reinstatement after violation**: if a user cures a violation within 30 days of discovery, their rights are automatically reinstated — a remedy absent in prior versions [3][5].

## References
[1] Creative Commons. "CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 Deed." https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
[2] Creative Commons. "CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 Legal Code." https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/legalcode
[3] Creative Commons. "What's New in 4.0." https://creativecommons.org/version4/
[4] Creative Commons Wiki. "4.0 Improvements." https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/4.0
[5] Kreutzer, T. "Open Content – A Practical Guide to Using Creative Commons Licences." https://meta.wikimedia.org/wiki/Open_Content_-_A_Practical_Guide_to_Using_Creative_Commons_Licences
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the entity (Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International) and provided the correct canonical URL, so both pass the DEEP logic check. On WIDTH, both covered core freedoms (Share, Adapt), primary conditions (Attribution, NonCommercial, ShareAlike, no additional restrictions), key disclaimers (no warranties, liability limits, treatment of moral/publicity rights), and multiple major 4.0 improvements (internationalization, database rights, attribution flexibility, moral rights clarification, automatic reinstatement). Neither mentioned the deed-not-a-license or CC-not-a-law-firm disclaimer explicitly, so they are comparable on minor omissions. 

On User Utility & Presentation, Response B is slightly more comprehensive and better grounded. It references both the deed and legal code, includes more detailed disclaimer language (e.g., trademark/patent rights, explicit reinstatement mechanics), and cites a broader set of sources (including the legal code and version 4.0 materials). Structurally, both are clear and scannable, but B provides marginally richer legal nuance and citation density, enhancing credibility. 

Since both are factually strong and complete, the decision rests on formatting depth and informational richness. Therefore, B is better on user experience grounds, though A is also high quality.
